title: Surface Area & Volume
blurb: Prisms, cylinders, pyramids, cones, and spheres — wrapping and filling 3-D solids, then combining them into composite figures.
buildsOn: Area formulas from Unit 10; the Pythagorean theorem (Unit 8) for slant heights; circle measures from Unit 9.

lessons.title: Prisms: Surface Area & Volume
lessons.concreteComparison: A cereal box, a brick, a stick of butter — all prisms. To find how much cereal fits inside you need its **volume**; to find how much cardboard wraps it you need its **surface area**. Volume fills the space; surface area covers the outside.
lessons.objective: Find the volume (base area × height) and the surface area of a prism, using a net to see all the faces.
concept: A **prism** has two identical parallel **bases** joined by rectangular **lateral faces**. A box is a **rectangular prism**, and a **cube** is a rectangular prism whose length, width, and height are all equal.
**Volume** measures how much space a solid fills, in **cubic units**. For any prism, $V = (\text{base area}) \times (\text{height})$; for a box, $V = l \times w \times h$.
**Surface area** is the total area of all the faces, in **square units**. A **net** unfolds the solid flat so you can add up every face.
A rectangular prism's six faces come in three matching pairs, so $SA = 2(lw + lh + wh)$.
lessons.keyIdea: Prism volume = base area × height (cubic units). Surface area = the sum of all faces; for a box, SA = 2(lw + lh + wh).

facts.title: Prism volume
facts.explanation: Base area B times the height h.

workedExamples.statement: A box is $2 \times 3 \times 4$. Find its volume.
Find the surface area of the same $2 \times 3 \times 4$ box.

commonMistakes.text: Mixing up cubic and square units.
commonMistakes.correction: **Volume** is in **cubic** units ($\text{cm}^3$); **surface area** is in **square** units ($\text{cm}^2$).

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting a pair of faces in surface area.
commonMistakes.correction: A box has **6** faces in **3 pairs** — include all of them: $2(lw + lh + wh)$.

steps.title: Fill it or wrap it
beats: A **prism** has two identical **bases** joined by rectangular sides.
**Volume** = how much space it fills (cubic units).
**Surface area** = the total area of its outside faces (square units).
A box (rectangular prism) is the simplest example.

steps.title: Volume = base × height
steps.teach: A prism's volume is the **base area** times the **height**. For this box, $V = l \times w \times h$. Change the dimensions and watch the volume (and surface area) update.

steps.title: Base area times height
beats: $V = (\text{base area}) \times (\text{height})$.
For a box, the base is $l \times w$, so $V = lwh$.
$2 \times 3 \times 4 = 24$ cubic units.
This works for ANY prism — just find the base area first.

examples.prompt: A prism with base area $15$ and height $6$. Volume?

steps.title: The net shows every face
steps.teach: Unfold the box flat and you see all 6 faces. Add them up for the surface area — they come in 3 matching pairs: $2(lw + lh + wh)$. Here $2(12 + 20 + 15) = 94$.

steps.title: Add up the faces
beats: **Surface area** = the total area of all the faces.
A box has 6 faces in 3 pairs; a **cube** is a box with all sides equal, so all 6 faces match.
$SA = 2(lw + lh + wh)$.
$2(6 + 8 + 12) = 52$ for a $2 \times 3 \times 4$ box.

problems.prompt: Surface area is measured in — units.

steps.title: Why big animals lose heat slowly
steps.body: Volume grows faster than surface area as things get bigger (cubes vs squares). A large animal has lots of volume making heat but relatively little skin to lose it through — which is why elephants overheat and mice must eat constantly to stay warm. It's the surface-area-to-volume ratio from this lesson.

steps.title: Box volume
steps.prompt: A rectangular box measures $5 \times 6 \times 2$. What is its volume?

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Prism volume** $= (\text{base area}) \times (\text{height})$; for a box, $V = lwh$ (cubic units).
**Surface area** = the total of all faces; a box is $2(lw + lh + wh)$ (square units).
A **net** unfolds the solid so you can add every face.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's find prism volumes and surface areas.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Volume uses — units; surface area uses — units.

steps.title: You've finished "Prisms: Surface Area & Volume"
steps.body: You can fill and wrap any prism. Next: cylinders — prisms with a circular base.

lessons.title: Cylinders
lessons.concreteComparison: A soup can is a cylinder. Its volume is the circular base times the height; its surface is two circle lids plus the label wrapped around — a rectangle exactly as wide as the circle's circumference.
lessons.objective: Find the volume (πr²h) and surface area (2πr² + 2πrh) of a cylinder.
concept: A **cylinder** is like a prism, but its base is a **circle** instead of a polygon.
**Volume** $= (\text{base area}) \times h = \pi r^2 h$ — the circle area times the height.
**Surface area** $= $ two circles $+$ the wrapped label: $2\pi r^2 + 2\pi r h$. The label is a rectangle $2\pi r$ wide (the **circumference**) by $h$ tall.
Leave answers **in terms of $\pi$** unless a decimal is asked for.
lessons.keyIdea: Cylinder volume = πr²h (circle base × height). Surface area = 2πr² (two circles) + 2πrh (the wrapped label of width 2πr, height h).

facts.title: Cylinder surface area
facts.explanation: Two circles plus the wrapped rectangle.

workedExamples.statement: A cylinder has radius $3$ and height $5$. Find its volume (in terms of π).
Find its surface area (in terms of π).

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting to square the radius in the volume.
commonMistakes.correction: Volume uses the **circle area** $\pi r^2$, so the radius is **squared**: $\pi r^2 h$.

commonMistakes.text: Leaving out the two circle ends in surface area.
commonMistakes.correction: Surface area is the label ($2\pi r h$) **plus** the two circle lids ($2\pi r^2$).

steps.title: A can-shaped solid
beats: A **cylinder** is a prism with a **circle** for its base.
**Volume** = circle area × height = $\pi r^2 h$.
**Surface area** = two circle ends + the wrapped side.
The wrapped side is a rectangle as wide as the circumference.

steps.title: Volume = circle × height
steps.teach: The volume is the circle base ($\pi r^2$) times the height $h$: $V = \pi r^2 h$. Radius $3$, height $5$ gives $45\pi$. Change the dimensions.

steps.title: πr² times h
beats: Base area is the circle: $\pi r^2$.
$V = \pi r^2 h$.
Radius $3$, height $5$: $\pi(9)(5) = 45\pi$.
The radius is squared; the height is not.

problems.prompt: Cylinder volume uses the — of the base.

steps.title: Unwrap the surface
steps.teach: Unroll a cylinder and you get two circles ($\pi r^2$ each) and a rectangle. The rectangle is $2\pi r$ wide (the circumference) by $h$ tall, area $2\pi r h$. Total $SA = 2\pi r^2 + 2\pi r h$.

steps.title: Two circles + a wrapped rectangle
beats: Two circle ends: $2 \times \pi r^2 = 2\pi r^2$.
The label unrolls to a rectangle $2\pi r$ wide, $h$ tall: $2\pi r h$.
$SA = 2\pi r^2 + 2\pi r h$.
Radius $3$, height $5$: $18\pi + 30\pi = 48\pi$.

problems.prompt: The wrapped label's width equals the circle's —

steps.title: Why cans are the shape they are
steps.body: For a fixed volume, a can shaped so its height equals its diameter uses the least metal — the minimum surface area. Real cans are a bit taller than that ideal because the top and bottom seams need extra material, but the trade-off between volume and surface area is exactly this lesson's math, run by every packaging engineer.

steps.title: Cylinder volume
steps.prompt: A cylinder has radius $2$ and height $9$. What is its volume (in terms of π)?

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Cylinder volume** $= \pi r^2 h$ — circle base times height.
**Surface area** $= 2\pi r^2 + 2\pi r h$ — two circles plus the wrapped rectangle.
The label's width is the **circumference** $2\pi r$.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's find cylinder volumes and surface areas in terms of π.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

steps.title: You've finished "Cylinders"
steps.body: You can fill and wrap a cylinder. Next: pyramids — which hold exactly a THIRD of their prism.

lessons.title: Pyramids
lessons.concreteComparison: The Great Pyramid of Giza, a tent, a glass paperweight — a pyramid narrows from a flat base up to a single point, the apex. Its volume is exactly ONE THIRD of the prism with the same base and height.
lessons.objective: Find the volume of a pyramid (⅓ × base area × height) and describe its surface area (base + triangular faces).
concept: A **pyramid** has a polygon **base** and triangular faces that meet at a single point, the **apex**.
**Volume** $= \tfrac13 (\text{base area}) \times h$ — exactly **one third** of the prism with the same base and height.
The **height** $h$ is the perpendicular distance from the apex straight down to the base (not the slanted edge).
**Surface area** $= $ the base $+$ the triangular **lateral faces**, and each triangle's height is the **slant height** of the pyramid.
lessons.keyIdea: Pyramid volume = ⅓ × base area × height — one third of the matching prism. Surface area = base + the triangular lateral faces.

facts.title: Pyramid volume
facts.explanation: One third of base area × height.

workedExamples.statement: A pyramid has a square base of side $6$ and height $10$. Find its volume.
A pyramid with base area $27$ and height $5$. Find its volume.

commonMistakes.correction: A pyramid is **one third** of its prism. Always multiply by $\tfrac13$ (or divide by 3).

commonMistakes.text: Using the slant edge as the height.
commonMistakes.correction: The **height** is the perpendicular distance apex-to-base; the slanted edge is longer.

steps.title: Narrowing to a point
beats: A **pyramid** has a flat **base** and triangular sides.
They meet at a single point, the **apex**.
The **height** goes straight from the apex down to the base.
Its volume is a fraction of the matching prism.

steps.title: A third of the prism
steps.teach: A pyramid holds exactly **one third** the volume of the prism with the same base and height: $V = \tfrac13(\text{base area})h$. Square base $6$, height $5$: $\tfrac13(36)(5) = 60$.

steps.title: One third of base × height
beats: $V = \tfrac13 (\text{base area}) \times h$.
Three pyramids exactly fill their prism.
Square base $6$, height $10$: base area $36$, $V = \tfrac13(360) = 120$.
Find the base area first, then take a third.

problems.prompt: A pyramid's volume is — the prism with the same base and height.

steps.title: Perpendicular, not slanted
beats: The volume uses the **perpendicular height** — apex straight down to the base.
The **slant height** (down a triangular face) is longer.
Slant height is used for **surface area**, not volume.
Keep the two straight.

problems.prompt: The surface area of a pyramid uses the — height.
problems.explanation: Slant height for the triangular faces.

steps.title: Three pyramids fill a cube
steps.body: You can cut a cube into exactly three identical square pyramids, each with the cube's base and height — a hands-on proof that a pyramid is one third of its prism. Ancient builders and modern packaging designers both rely on this $\tfrac13$ to estimate material and fill.

steps.title: Pyramid volume
steps.prompt: A pyramid has a square base of side $5$ and height $9$. What is its volume?

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Pyramid volume** $= \tfrac13 (\text{base area}) \times h$ — one third of the matching prism.
The **height** is perpendicular (apex to base); the **slant height** is for surface area.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's find pyramid volumes — base area, then a third.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: A pyramid is — of its prism.
The perpendicular height of a pyramid runs from the apex to —

steps.title: You've finished "Pyramids"
steps.body: You can find any pyramid's volume. Next: cones — round pyramids with a circular base.

lessons.title: Cones
lessons.concreteComparison: An ice-cream cone, a party hat, a traffic cone — a cone narrows from a circular base up to a single point. Just like a pyramid is a third of its prism, a cone is exactly ONE THIRD of the cylinder with the same base and height.
lessons.objective: Find the volume (⅓πr²h) and surface area (πr² + πrℓ) of a cone, and find the slant height with the Pythagorean theorem.
concept: A **cone** is like a pyramid, but its base is a **circle**. It narrows from the base to a single point, the **apex**.
**Volume** $= \tfrac13 \pi r^2 h$ — exactly **one third** of the cylinder with the same base and height.

The **slant height** $\ell$ runs from the apex down the side to the edge of the base. It is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs $r$ and $h$, so $\ell = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$ (Pythagoras, from Unit 8).
**Surface area** $= \pi r^2$ (the circular base) $+ \pi r \ell$ (the curved side).
lessons.keyIdea: Cone volume = ⅓πr²h — a third of its cylinder. Surface area = πr² + πrℓ, where the slant height ℓ = √(r² + h²).

workedExamples.statement: A cone has radius $3$ and height $4$. Find its volume (in terms of π).
The same cone (radius $3$, height $4$). Find its slant height, then its surface area.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting the $\tfrac13$ in the cone volume.
commonMistakes.correction: A cone is **one third** of its cylinder: $V = \tfrac13 \pi r^2 h$, not $\pi r^2 h$.

commonMistakes.text: Using the height instead of the slant height for surface area.
commonMistakes.correction: The curved side uses the **slant height** $\ell = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$, not the vertical height $h$.

steps.title: A round pyramid
beats: A **cone** narrows from a **circular** base to a point.
It is the round version of a pyramid.
**Volume** = one third of its cylinder: $\tfrac13 \pi r^2 h$.
The **slant height** runs down the side to the base edge.

steps.title: A third of the cylinder
steps.teach: A cone holds exactly **one third** the volume of the cylinder with the same base and height: $V = \tfrac13 \pi r^2 h$. Radius $3$, height $4$: $\tfrac13\pi(9)(4) = 12\pi$. The readout also shows the slant height ℓ.

steps.title: One third of πr²h
beats: $V = \tfrac13 \pi r^2 h$.
Three cones exactly fill their cylinder.
Radius $3$, height $4$: $\tfrac13\pi(9)(4) = 12\pi$.
Find $\pi r^2 h$ first, then take a third.

problems.prompt: A cone is — the cylinder with the same base and height.

steps.title: Slant height + surface area
steps.teach: For the surface area you need the **slant height** ℓ — the distance down the side. It is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs $r$ and $h$: $\ell = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$. Here $\sqrt{9+16} = 5$, so $SA = \pi r^2 + \pi r\ell = 9\pi + 15\pi = 24\pi$.

steps.title: Pythagoras on r and h
beats: The slant height, radius, and height form a **right triangle**.
Legs $r$ and $h$; hypotenuse $\ell$.
$\ell = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$ (Unit 8).

problems.prompt: The curved side of a cone uses the — height.

steps.title: An hourglass of sand
steps.body: Fill a cone-shaped cup and pour it into a cylinder of the same radius and height — you'll need to fill and pour exactly three times to fill the cylinder. That physical fact is the $\tfrac13$ in the cone (and pyramid) volume formula, and it's how ancient granaries estimated stored grain.

steps.title: Cone volume
steps.prompt: A cone has radius $2$ and height $6$. What is its volume (in terms of π)?

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Cone volume** $= \tfrac13 \pi r^2 h$ — one third of its cylinder.
**Surface area** $= \pi r^2 + \pi r \ell$ — base circle plus curved side.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's find cone volumes, slant heights, and surface areas.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: A cone's volume is — of its cylinder.

steps.title: You've finished "Cones"
steps.body: You can fill and wrap a cone. Next: spheres — the roundest solid of all.

lessons.title: Spheres
lessons.concreteComparison: A basketball, a globe, a drop of water — a sphere is the set of all points the same distance (the radius) from a center. It's the most symmetric solid there is, and its volume and surface area depend on nothing but the radius.
lessons.objective: Find the volume (4⁄3πr³) and surface area (4πr²) of a sphere, and handle a hemisphere as half a sphere.
concept: A **sphere** is the set of all points a fixed distance — the **radius** $r$ — from a **center**. Think of a ball.
**Volume** $= \tfrac43 \pi r^3$. The radius is **cubed**, so doubling the radius multiplies the volume by $8$.
**Surface area** $= 4 \pi r^2$ — exactly four times the area of a flat circle with the same radius.
A **hemisphere** is half a sphere, so its volume is $\tfrac12 \cdot \tfrac43 \pi r^3 = \tfrac23 \pi r^3$.
lessons.keyIdea: Sphere volume = 4⁄3πr³ (radius cubed). Surface area = 4πr² (four circles). A hemisphere is half a sphere: ⅔πr³.

workedExamples.statement: A sphere has radius $3$. Find its volume (in terms of π).
The same sphere (radius $3$). Find its surface area.

commonMistakes.text: Squaring the radius in the volume instead of cubing it.
commonMistakes.correction: Volume is 3-D: the radius is **cubed** — $\tfrac43 \pi r^3$. Surface area squares it.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting the $4$ in the surface area.
commonMistakes.correction: Sphere surface area is $4\pi r^2$ — four circles' worth, not one.

steps.title: The roundest solid
beats: A **sphere** is all points a fixed distance $r$ from a center.
Everything about it depends only on the **radius**.
**Volume** $= \tfrac43 \pi r^3$ (radius cubed).
**Surface area** $= 4\pi r^2$ (radius squared).

steps.title: Volume = 4⁄3πr³
steps.teach: A sphere's volume is $\tfrac43\pi r^3$ — the radius is **cubed**. Radius $3$: $\tfrac43\pi(27) = 36\pi$. Because of the cube, doubling the radius makes the volume $8$ times as big.

steps.title: Four thirds π r cubed
beats: $V = \tfrac43 \pi r^3$.
Cube the radius, then multiply by $\tfrac43\pi$.
Doubling $r$ multiplies volume by $2^3 = 8$.

problems.prompt: In the sphere volume the radius is —

steps.title: Surface area = 4πr²
steps.teach: A sphere's surface area is $4\pi r^2$ — exactly four times a flat circle of the same radius. Radius $3$: $4\pi(9) = 36\pi$. (For this radius the volume and surface area happen to share the number $36\pi$ — a coincidence, not a rule.)

steps.title: Four flat circles
beats: That's four times the area of a circle with radius $r$.
Surface area **squares** the radius; volume cubes it.

problems.prompt: A hemisphere (half a sphere) of radius $3$ has volume —

steps.title: Why bubbles are round
steps.body: A soap bubble holds a fixed volume of air with the least possible surface, and the sphere is the unique shape that wins that contest — the most volume for the least surface area. Surface tension pulls every bubble toward that minimum, which is why free-floating bubbles are always spheres.

steps.title: Sphere volume
steps.prompt: A sphere has radius $3$. What is its volume (in terms of π)?

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Surface area** $= 4\pi r^2$ — four flat circles' worth.
A **hemisphere** is half a sphere: $\tfrac23 \pi r^3$.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's find sphere volumes and surface areas in terms of π.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

steps.title: You've finished "Spheres"
steps.body: You can fill and wrap a sphere. Next: composite solids — combining everything you've learned.

lessons.title: Composite Solids & Applications
lessons.concreteComparison: An ice-cream cone topped with a scoop, a silo that is a cylinder capped by a dome, a pencil (cylinder + cone) — real objects are built from simple solids joined together. To find the total volume, break the object into pieces, find each piece, and add (or subtract for a hollow).
lessons.objective: Find the volume and surface area of composite solids by decomposing them into prisms, cylinders, pyramids, cones, and spheres.
concept: A **composite solid** is made of two or more simple solids joined together.
**Volume of a composite** = add the volumes of the pieces. For a **hollow** (a hole or a scoop removed), **subtract** the missing piece.
**Surface area of a composite** is trickier: only count the faces on the **outside**. Where two pieces join, those faces are hidden and don't count.
The method never changes: **identify each piece, apply its formula, then combine.** Every formula from this unit is a tool you now have.
lessons.keyIdea: Break a composite solid into simple pieces, find each volume, and add — or subtract a removed piece. For surface area, count only the outside faces.

workedExamples.statement: A silo is a cylinder of radius $3$ and height $10$ topped by a hemisphere of radius $3$. Find the total volume (in terms of π).
A block is a $5 \times 4 \times 6$ box with a $2 \times 4 \times 6$ rectangular notch cut out. Find the remaining volume.

commonMistakes.text: Counting a hidden face where two solids join.
commonMistakes.correction: For **surface area**, drop the faces that touch inside; only the **outside** is exposed.
A **hole or scoop removed** means **subtract** that piece's volume from the whole.

tryIt.statement: A cylinder (r $2$, h $5$) topped by a cone (r $2$, h $3$). Total volume?
A $4 \times 4 \times 4$ cube with a $2 \times 2 \times 4$ hole drilled through. Remaining volume?

steps.title: Solids made of solids
beats: A **composite solid** is built from simple pieces joined together.
A silo = cylinder + dome; a pencil = cylinder + cone.
**Volume**: find each piece, then **add** (subtract a hollow).
The formulas are all ones you already know.

steps.title: Piece 1: the cylinder
steps.teach: Picture a grain silo: a cylinder with a dome on top. First the cylinder — radius $3$, height $10$: $V = \pi(9)(10) = 90\pi$. We'll add the dome next.

steps.title: Piece 2: the dome
steps.teach: The dome is a **hemisphere** of radius $3$ — half a sphere: $\tfrac12 \cdot \tfrac43\pi(27) = 18\pi$. Add it to the cylinder: $90\pi + 18\pi = 108\pi$ total.

steps.title: Decompose, compute, combine
beats: Cylinder: $\pi(9)(10) = 90\pi$.
Hemisphere: $\tfrac23\pi(27) = 18\pi$.
Total volume $= 90\pi + 18\pi = 108\pi$.
Identify each piece, apply its formula, add.

examples.prompt: Cylinder (r $2$, h $5$) + cone (r $2$, h $3$) on top. Total?

problems.prompt: Cube (side $4$) + square pyramid (base $4$, height $3$) on top. Total?
To combine two joined solids' volumes you —
problems.explanation: Add the pieces (subtract only a hollow).

steps.title: Holes and scoops
beats: When a piece is **removed**, subtract its volume.
A box with a hole drilled through: box $-$ hole.
$5\times4\times6 = 120$, notch $2\times4\times6 = 48$, left $= 72$.
Same idea, opposite sign.

examples.prompt: $5 \times 4 \times 6$ box with a $2 \times 4 \times 6$ notch removed. Remaining?
Cube side $4$ with a $2 \times 2 \times 4$ hole drilled through. Remaining?

problems.prompt: A $10 \times 10 \times 2$ slab with a $4 \times 4 \times 2$ square hole. Remaining?
For a scoop removed from a solid, you — its volume.

steps.title: The surface-area catch
steps.body: When you glue two solids together, the faces where they touch vanish from the OUTSIDE — so a composite's surface area is less than the sum of its parts. That's why engineers designing a fuel tank (cylinder + two hemispheres) never just add the whole formulas: the flat circle ends where the caps meet the tube are hidden and don't count.

steps.title: Composite volume
steps.prompt: A cylinder (radius $2$, height $6$) is topped by a cone (radius $2$, height $9$). What is the total volume (in terms of π)?

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **composite solid** breaks into simple pieces — find each, then combine.
**Add** joined pieces; **subtract** a hole or scoop that's removed.
For **surface area**, count only the faces on the outside.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's decompose composite solids and combine the pieces.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Cube side $4$ + square pyramid (base $4$, height $3$). Volume?
$5 \times 4 \times 6$ box with a $2 \times 4 \times 6$ notch removed. Remaining?
When two solids are joined, the touching faces are —
problems.explanation: Inside, so not part of the surface.

problems.prompt: Cube side $4$ with a $2 \times 2 \times 4$ hole drilled through. Remaining?

steps.title: You've finished "Surface Area & Volume"
steps.body: You can now fill and wrap prisms, cylinders, pyramids, cones, spheres — and any solid built from them. That completes 3-D measurement. Next unit: Transformations.

practice.statement: A rectangular prism measures $8 \times 5 \times 4$. Find its volume and surface area.
A cylinder has radius $3$ and height $10$. Find its exact volume and total surface area.
A square pyramid has base edge $6$ and height $4$. Find its volume, and its surface area given slant height $5$.
A cone has radius $5$ and height $12$. Find its exact volume and total surface area.
Find the exact volume and surface area of a sphere with radius $3$.
practice.solution: $V = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = 36\pi$ and $SA = 4\pi r^2 = 36\pi$ — numerically equal at exactly $r = 3$.

practice.statement: A cube has edge $7$. Find its volume and surface area.
A silo is a cylinder (radius $2$, height $5$) topped by a hemisphere of radius $2$. Find its exact total volume.
Every edge of a prism is doubled. What happens to its surface area and its volume?
practice.solution: Lengths $\times 2$ ⟹ areas $\times 2^2 = 4$ and volumes $\times 2^3 = 8$. Skin quadruples; capacity octuples — the square-cube law.

practice.statement: A semicircle of radius $10$ is rolled into a cone (the straight edges glued). Find the cone's base radius and height.
practice.solution: The semicircle's arc, $\pi \cdot 10$, becomes the base circumference: $2\pi r = 10\pi$ gives $r = 5$. The slant is the semicircle's radius, $\ell = 10$, so $h = \sqrt{100 - 25} = 5\sqrt{3}$.

practice.statement: A right triangle with legs $3$ and $4$ is rotated about the leg of length $4$. Name the solid and find its volume.
practice.solution: A cone: radius $3$ (the spinning leg), height $4$. $V = \frac{1}{3}\pi \cdot 9 \cdot 4 = 12\pi$.

practice.statement: A cone has base radius $4$ and slant height $12$. Its lateral surface is cut open and flattened into a sector. Find the sector's central angle.
practice.solution: The sector's radius is the slant $12$; its arc is the base circumference $8\pi$. Angle $= 360° \cdot \frac{8\pi}{2\pi \cdot 12} = 360° \cdot \frac{4}{12} = 120°$.

testYourself.statement: A prism has base area $24$ and height $7$. Find its volume.
A cylinder of height $3$ has volume $75\pi$. Find its radius.
A pyramid has a square base of area $49$ and height $9$. Find its volume.
A cone has radius $9$ and slant height $15$. Find its height and exact volume.
A sphere has surface area $100\pi$. Find its radius and exact volume.
A solid hemisphere has radius $6$. Find its exact volume and its TOTAL surface area (curved part plus flat disk).
testYourself.solution: Volume: $\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{4}{3}\pi(216) = 144\pi$. Surface: half-sphere $2\pi r^2 = 72\pi$ plus the flat disk $\pi r^2 = 36\pi$, total $108\pi$.

testYourself.statement: A rectangle with sides $3$ and $5$ is rotated about its longer side. Name the solid and find its volume and total surface area.
testYourself.solution: A cylinder: radius $3$, height $5$. $V = \pi \cdot 9 \cdot 5 = 45\pi$. $S = 2\pi r h + 2\pi r^2 = 30\pi + 18\pi = 48\pi$.
